content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 20, 1990

Karen Bass, Accountant
Charles E. Bray, CPA
2626 Nicholson Street
San Leandro, CA 94577

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. A-90-474

Dear Ms. Bass:

You have requested advice on behalf of Sheriff Charles
Plummer concerning his duties under the new "personal use"
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

May the sheriff use campaign funds to pay for a trip to China
under the auspices of the Citizen Ambassador Program?

CONCLUSION

Since the trip to China is related to the sheriff's elective
office, it has a governmental purpose. Therefore, the use of
campaign funds for payments or reimbursements for the ordinary and
necessary expenses associated with the trip, including travel and
accommodations, is permissible. However, the Commission
interprets the applicable Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and
274 to allow the use of campaign funds only for the business
portions of the trip.

FACTS

Sheriff Charles C. Plummer of Alameda County has been invited
to visit China as a member of a small delegation (30) of North
American professional and citizens leaders involved in the

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-474
Page 2


administration of juvenile and criminal justice. The project is
being organized under the auspices of the Citizen Ambassador
Program. The Citizen Ambassador Program will coordinate travel
and professional arrangements with the program's hosts, the
Chinese Bar Association.

The delegation will convene in Beijing on October 9, will
visit Beijing, Shanghai, Hangzhou, and Hong Kong, and return to
San Francisco on October 23, 1990 (15 days). The estimated
expense per delegate of $3,175 includes all professional
activities, transportation within China, accommodations, all meals
in China, most meals in Hong Kong, and cultural and sightseeing
activities. Delegates are responsible for their own air
connections to Beijing and from Hong Kong.

ANALYSIS

Effective January 1, 1990, the Act was amended by Senate
Bill 1431 to include new provisions that regulate the appropriate
use of campaign funds. (Section 85800, et seq.) The purpose of
the legislation was "to ensure that candidates, elected officers
and the people clearly recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses
of campaign funds." (Senate Elections Committee Analysis of
SB 1431.)

The general rule of the new personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 85801.) However, where an expenditure confers a
substantial personal benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) A substantial personal
benefit is defined as a direct personal benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $100 or more. (Section 85800(b)(2).)

Moreover, the new personal use law sets forth provisions
dealing with specific types of expenditures. Section 85802(a)(1),
which deals with travel, provides:

     (a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay
or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
employees or staff of the campaign committee or the
elected officer's governmental agency for travel
expenses and necessary accommodations except when
these expenditures are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

     (1) For purposes of this section, payments or
reimbursements for travel and necessary accommoda-
tions shall be considered as directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose if

Our File No. A-90-474
Page 3


the payments would meet standards similar to the
standards of the Internal Revenue Service pursuant
to Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue
Code for deductions of travel expenses under the
federal income tax law.

(Emphasis added.)


According to Section 85802(a)(1), the use of campaign funds
for payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary accommoda-
tions is permissible if the payments meet standards similar to the
standards for the deduction of travel expenses pursuant to
Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and 274. The Commission
presently is considering regulations interpreting this section.
Thus, it is possible that the Commission may adopt regulations
which may be at variance with the advice given in this letter.
However, until the Commission adopts regulations, we are
interpreting Section 85802(a)(1) to require a political,
legislative or governmental purpose for the travel and
accommodations that are clearly associated with either the
elective office held or the elective office sought by the
official.2/ If there is a political, legislative or governmental
purpose, campaign funds may be used for ordinary and necessary
expenses associated with the travel and accommodation consistent
with the Internal Revenue Service standards.3/

Internal Revenue Code Section 162(a)(2) sets forth general
rules which permit the deduction of all ordinary and necessary
traveling expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade
or business. In light of the purpose behind the new personal use
law, we have interpreted the Internal Revenue Code Sections to
allow only for the use of campaign funds for the "business
portions" of the trip. Thus, the 15-day trip to China may be
broken into business days (days on which there are meetings and
field visits) and nonbusiness days. Where a weekend falls between
business days, the weekend will also be counted as business time.
By our calculations twelve-fifteenths of the fee for the trip may
be paid from the campaign funds of elected officials.


2/ Section 85202 provides that campaign funds are held in trust
for expenses associated with the office held or sought. Here,
such an association would appear to be present if the purpose of
the trip is to acquire information regarding alternative
enforcement techniques which may be adaptable for use in the
sheriff's jurisdiction.

3/ The Commission has not yet defined a "governmental purpose"
under the personal use sections. However, it appears from the
attached letter and proposed schedule of activities that the trip
is directly related to a governmental purpose.

Our File No. A-90-474
Page 4


However, no part of expenditures for solely personal
purposes, such as some of the meals, entertainment, guided
cultural and sight-seeing activities, may be paid for with
campaign funds. Consequently, since these personal expenses
appear to be part of the "package price," they must be subtracted
out of the total fee prior to the proration of the fee.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

Deanne Stone

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa

portions of the trip.

interprets the applicable Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and
